cuisine: Western / Mediterranean — widely consumed in Singapore across all ethnic communities
region_of_origin: Central Asia / Middle East / Eastern Europe (ancient origin); globally produced and consumed
food_subgroup: Full-fat fermented cow's milk yogurt (plain, unsweetened) — live active cultures of Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus

preparation_method: bacterial fermentation of whole cow's milk
incubation with live cultures (L. bulgaricus, S. thermophilus)
main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.description: 1 standard individual tub (150g) or 1 small bowl (100g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard individual tub of plain full-fat yogurt as sold in Singapore supermarkets (e.g., Meiji, Greenfields) is approximately 150g; a small bowl serving is approximately 100g.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Full-Fat Yogurt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171284) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) SR Legacy; Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA SR Legacy plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) and cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo database for plain full-fat yogurt. Values are consistent across both sources. Per-100g figures used directly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; plain full-fat yogurt GI ~36 (low)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain full-fat yogurt GI of 36 is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. GL per 150g serving = (36 × 4.7g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.5) / 100 = 2.5g. Low GI is attributable to the fat (3.3g/100g) and protein (5.3g/100g) content slowing gastric emptying, and the lactose matrix having lower glycaemic impact than glucose or sucrose.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (46mg/100g), high potassium (234mg/100g), calcium (121mg/100g), and ACE-inhibitory bioactive peptides from fermentation
hypertension.guidance: Plain full-fat yogurt is beneficial for hypertension management: its potassium-to-sodium ratio is highly favourable (5:1), and fermentation-derived ACE-inhibitory peptides (Val-Pro-Pro, Ile-Pro-Pro) have demonstrated modest blood-pressure-lowering effects in clinical trials; a 2018 meta-analysis of 31 prospective cohort studies (Journal of Hypertension) found dairy yogurt consumption associated with a 13% reduction in hypertension risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~36), probiotic-mediated insulin sensitivity improvement, and low carbohydrate content (4.7g/100g as lactose)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain full-fat yogurt is suitable and beneficial for Type 2 Diabetes: its GI of ~36 produces minimal postprandial glycaemic excursion, the 4.7g carbohydrate per 100g is entirely lactose (metabolically less impactful than glucose or sucrose), and large cohort studies (Nurses Health Study, Health Professionals Follow-up Study) show 2+ servings per week of yogurt is associated with a 28% lower risk of T2DM incidence; probiotic cultures modulate gut microbiome to improve insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.1g/100g), beneficial potassium, calcium, and probiotic effects on lipid metabolism
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain full-fat yogurt presents low cardiovascular concern: while it contains 2.1g saturated fat per 100g, the food matrix effect of dairy (as opposed to isolated saturated fat) is associated with neutral-to-beneficial cardiovascular outcomes in prospective cohort data; potassium (234mg/100g) supports vascular health, and probiotic cultures may modestly improve HDL cholesterol and reduce LDL oxidation; a 150g serving contributes only 3.15g saturated fat, well within daily limits for most adults.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic gut microbiome modulation, calcium for bone protection in glucocorticoid-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain full-fat yogurt is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients: probiotic cultures modulate gut microbiome diversity relevant to the gut-joint axis and systemic inflammation, while calcium (182mg per 150g serving) is critical for RA patients on long-term glucocorticoids who face elevated osteoporosis risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain full-fat yogurt (酸奶, Thayir, Dahi) is a minimally processed dairy product made by fermenting whole cow's milk with live bacterial cultures — primarily Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus. The result is a thick, creamy, mildly tangy product with a smooth texture and a clean sour-dairy flavour. It contains no added sugar, flavouring, or thickeners in its natural form. In Singapore, plain full-fat yogurt is widely available from Meiji (Japanese brand with strong Singapore market share), Greenfields (Indonesian fresh dairy brand), Marigold, and NTUC FairPrice house brands. It is consumed as a standalone breakfast food (often topped with fruit and granola), as a post-exercise snack, and as a cooling accompaniment to Indian meals (functionally equivalent to dahi or curd — used in raita, served alongside biryani, or as a digestive aid with spicy dishes). Nutritionally, plain full-fat yogurt is excellent: it provides 5.3g complete protein per 100g (all essential amino acids), 121mg calcium per 100g (supporting bone health), a low glycemic index of ~36 (minimal postprandial glucose impact), and live probiotic cultures that support gut microbiome diversity and immune function. The primary clinical concern in Singapore is consumer confusion between plain yogurt (nutritionally beneficial) and flavoured sweetened yogurt (often containing 15–25g added sugar per serving, which is metabolically problematic for diabetes and weight management). The yogurt category in Singapore supermarkets is nutritionally heterogeneous — from near-zero-sugar plain yogurt to dessert-level sweetened fruit yogurt. For clinical and dietary guidance purposes, plain unsweetened full-fat yogurt should be clearly distinguished from sweetened variants. Key nutrients per 100g: energy 61 kcal, protein 5.3g, fat 3.3g (saturated 2.1g), carbohydrate 4.7g (all as lactose), sodium 46mg, potassium 234mg, calcium 121mg, vitamin B12 0.37mcg.
common_user_queries: full fat yogurt vs low fat yogurt

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 171284) for plain whole-milk yogurt and cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo database. GI value of 36 is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified against their respective databases. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Record is complete across all required sections.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — plain yogurt contains no dietary fibre as it is a liquid-fermented dairy product with no plant-cell-wall material. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy (naturally occurring ruminant trans fats such as CLA are present at trace levels below reporting threshold and are not the industrially produced trans fats of clinical concern). monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1g) are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171284. water_g (85.1g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. The 150g serving size reflects the standard individual tub size for commercial plain yogurt in Singapore (Meiji, Greenfields). GL per 150g serving calculated as: (GI 36 × carbohydrate 4.7g/100g × 1.5) / 100 = 2.5g. Note: 'Greek yogurt' is listed as an alias for retrieval purposes only — Greek yogurt is a strained variant with higher protein and lower carbohydrate than standard plain full-fat yogurt; users searching 'Greek yogurt' may be redirected to this record but should be advised of the nutritional differences. The input record listed 'diabetes' as the condition key; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
